does: This paper introduces AgentShield, a detection framework for identifying indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks in tool-using LLM agents after compromise occurs rather than preventing attacks upfront. The core method deploys three types of traps within the agent's tool interface—fake tools, fake credentials, and allowlisted parameters—that compromised agents nearly always trigger when following hidden attacker instructions. These trap triggers simultaneously serve as high-precision labels for training a self-supervised classifier without manual annotation. The framework is evaluated across 176 cross-lingual attack prompts in multiple languages including low-resource languages like Kurdish and Arabic, tested against four LLMs from three different providers, achieving effective detection of the small percentage of successful IPI attacks that slip through existing defenses.
connects: This work addresses fundamental limitations in the existing IPI defense literature, which has focused almost exclusively on prevention strategies evaluated primarily in English. It builds on prior research recognizing that even state-of-the-art prompt injection mitigations fail to stop all attacks, and extends the growing body of work on adversarial robustness in LLM agent systems. The paper connects to research on self-supervised learning and automated label generation, leveraging the insight that deception mechanisms can simultaneously serve defensive and labeling purposes. It also responds to the critical gap in cross-lingual AI safety research by systematically evaluating attacks and defenses across linguistically diverse prompts, complementing earlier work that implicitly assumed English-centric threat models.
enables: AgentShield opens new research directions in post-compromise detection for AI agents, shifting the defensive paradigm from prevention-only to defense-in-depth strategies. The trap-based approach and self-supervised labeling method could generalize to other agent vulnerabilities and multi-step attack scenarios. The cross-lingual evaluation framework and findings about language-specific attack patterns enable future work on building inclusive AI safety practices that protect speakers of low-resource languages. The paper points toward hybrid defense systems combining prevention, detection, and mitigation, and suggests that deception-based security mechanisms could be more practical for real-world deployment than perfect prevention. It also motivates further research into how agents interact with tool interfaces under adversarial conditions.
